titleEn: SDSU researchers develop vaccine for destructive poultry virus
titleZh: 南达科他州立大学研发出针对破坏性禽类病毒的疫苗
summaryZh: 南达科他州立大学动物疾病研究与诊断实验室成功研发出针对禽偏肺病毒（aMPV）B亚型的活疫苗和灭活疫苗候选株。该病毒在2024年首次在美国大规模爆发，导致明尼苏达州损失220万只火鸡，经济损失约1.12亿美元。研究团队采用中传代候选株在鸡和商业火鸡中实现了完全病毒清除，后续将进行临床前评估和田间试验。
featuredReason: 该疫苗研发直接针对2024年首次在美国大规模爆发的aMPV B亚型，对禽类产业具有重大经济保护意义，智慧畜牧行业人士可关注其后续田间试验及与监测系统的结合潜力。
contentFull: Health & disease Diseases Treatments and prevention SDSU researchers develop vaccine for destructive poultry virus Minnesota alone lost 2.2 million turkeys to the virus in 2024 11 June 2026 1 minute read By: Global Ag Media North America Researchers at South Dakota State University's Animal Disease Research and Diagnostic Laboratory have developed both live and killed vaccine candidates for avian metapneumovirus (aMPV) subtype B, a respiratory virus that has severely hampered US poultry production, the university announced.The team, led by assistant professors Sunil Mor and Tamer Sharafeldin, was the first to identify a fast-spreading respiratory viral outbreak affecting turkeys and chickens in major US poultry-producing regions in early 2024. While limited outbreaks of aMPV subtype C had occurred previously, it was the first time subtypes A and B — the two most pathogenic strains — had been identified in the US."Emerging subtypes A and B are causing huge economic losses to the poultry industry," said Mor. "We are expediting our efforts to provide the first vaccine based on a US field strain that will provide better protection to birds hence will be helpful in sustainability of US poultry production."The team developed three live vaccine candidates representing low-, medium- and high-passage attenuation, tested in chickens to evaluate safety and efficacy. The medium-passage candidate demonstrated the best protection, including complete viral clearance. Both live and killed vaccines also demonstrated significant safety and efficacy in commercial turkeys. Future experimental tests and field trials will confirm efficacy on subtype A."Developing vaccines is not going to be the only objective," said Sharafeldin. "Continued monitoring of new variants due to vaccine pressure will ensure the preparedness for any future outbreak due to emerging variants."The research, funded by the US Poultry and Egg Association and USPoultry Foundation, will now move to preclinical evaluation and field testing. In Minnesota alone, producers lost an estimated 2.2 million turkeys to aMPV in 2024, amounting to around $112 million in economic damages, according to the Minnesota Turkey Growers Association. Our Partners World Poultry Science Association Congress Poultry Science Association Targan dsm-firmenich Lallemand Lanxess BioZyme Hy-Line International Global Ag Media More from this author More News View all Chicken sticks enter US snack market as turkey output falls Markets & policy Market trends 2 July 2026 1 minute read WOAH reports 2.16 million birds lost to HPAI in May Health & disease Diseases Avian influenza 1 July 2026 1 minute read Europe worst affected as outbreaks spread across seven countries Canadian firms launch world's earliest in-ovo egg sexing system In-ovo sexing 1 July 2026 1 minute read Hyperspectral technology determines sex from day four of incubation Our Partners World Poultry Science Association Congress Poultry Science Association Targan dsm-firmenich Lallemand Lanxess BioZyme Hy-Line International
translationZh:
南达科他州立大学动物疾病研究与诊断实验室的研究人员已研发出针对禽偏肺病毒（aMPV）B亚型的活疫苗和灭活疫苗候选株，该病毒是一种严重阻碍美国禽类生产的呼吸道病毒，大学方面宣布。

由助理教授Sunil Mor和Tamer Sharafeldin领导的团队，于2024年初首次识别出影响美国主要禽类产区火鸡和鸡的快速传播呼吸道病毒爆发。虽然此前曾发生过aMPV C亚型的有限爆发，但这是A亚型和B亚型——两种致病性最强的毒株——首次在美国被确认。

"新出现的A亚型和B亚型正给禽类产业造成巨大的经济损失，"Mor表示。"我们正在加快努力，提供首个基于美国田间毒株的疫苗，这将为禽类提供更好的保护，从而有助于美国禽类生产的可持续性。"

该团队开发了三种活疫苗候选株，分别代表低、中、高传代减毒，并在鸡中进行了安全性和有效性测试。中传代候选株表现出最佳保护效果，包括完全病毒清除。活疫苗和灭活疫苗在商业火鸡中也显示出显著的安全性和有效性。未来的实验测试和田间试验将确认对A亚型的有效性。

**开发疫苗并非唯一目标**

"开发疫苗并非唯一目标，"Sharafeldin表示。"持续监测因疫苗压力而产生的新变异株，将确保为未来因新变异株引发的任何疫情做好准备。"

这项由美国禽蛋协会和USPoultry基金会资助的研究，将进入临床前评估和田间测试阶段。据明尼苏达火鸡养殖者协会称，仅明尼苏达州一地，2024年生产者因aMPV损失了约220万只火鸡，经济损失约1.12亿美元。
contentHtml:
SDSU researchers develop vaccine for destructive poultry virus 
Minnesota alone lost 2.2 million turkeys to the virus in 2024
